1993 CBR Scientific Director releases the 1st family banked sample for transplant.

1996 CBR launches the 1st program to offer free banking services to families with medical need.

1998 CBR is the 1st cord blood bank accredited by the American Association of Blood Banks.

2004 CBR releases the 1st unit in the world to be used by a child for a brain injury.

2005 CBR builds a new state-of-the-art lab in Tucson.

2010 CBR is the 1st to offer cord tissue banking to families in the U.S.

2010 CBR collaborates with Georgia Health Sciences University to initiate the first FDA-regulated clinical trial for cerebral palsy using a child’s own cord blood stem cells.

samples banked – and counting.

column5: Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.
column13:
COVID-19
Coronavirus Disease 2019

BE AWARE.

COVID-19 is a new respiratory illness that was first discovered in Wuhan, China. It is transmitted from person to person.

SYMPTOMS*

RUNNY NOSE
COUGH
SORE THROAT

COMMON SYMPTOMS

HIGH FEVER
PNEUMONIA
SEVERE RESPIRATORY DISEASE

SEVERE CASES

*Symptoms may appear 1 to 12 days following exposure to the virus

Who is most at risk of becoming seriously ill?
• People over age 60
• People with pre-existing conditions such as diabetes and heart disease

How is it transmitted?
• Through close contact with an infected person
• By an infected person coughing or sneezing
• By touching contaminated objects or surfaces and then touching your mouth, nose or eyes

Currently there is no vaccine or specific treatment. We can only treat the symptoms.
Serious cases may require oxygen and ventilatory support.

PREPARE.

Make sure you get your information from a reliable source.

Wash your hands regularly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based gel.

Cover your mouth with the inside of your elbow when you cough or sneeze or use a tissue and dispose of used tissue immediately and wash your hands.

ACT.

1M
Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.

Avoid touching your eyes, nose or mouth with unwashed hands.

Avoid sharing cups, plates or other personal items and disinfect all surfaces that are touched frequently.

If you have traveled to areas where COVID-19 is circulating or have been in contact with someone who has it and you experience fever, cough or difficulty breathing, seek medical attention immediately. Do not self-medicate.

Note: Information may change when more is known about the disease.
February 2020.

PAHO
Pan American Health Organization
World Health Organization
Americas

BE AWARE. PREPARE. ACT.
www.paho.org/coronavirus

TITLES/LOGLINES: The Avengers must band together to stop Thanos from collecting all six Infinity Stones.

THE INFINITY STONES

SPACE STONE
(Blue)
- Also known as: The Tesseract
- Powers: Control of space, portals across the cosmos.
- Appears In: Captain America: The First Avenger (as a prototype weapon), The Avengers (used by Loki), Thor: Ragnarok (briefly during events of the film), Captain Marvel (legacy of the Tesseract)
- Key Moments: Provides instantaneous travel and is sought by villains and heroes alike.

MIND STONE
(Yellow)
- Also known as: Loki's Scepter / Vision's Stone
- Powers: Mind control, consciousness, and sentience.
- Appears In: The Avengers (Loki's scepter), Avengers: Age of Ultron (creates Vision), Captain America: Civil War (in Vision)
- Key Moments: Used to create Vision; capable of controlling minds.

REALITY STONE
(Red)
- Also known as: The Aether
- Powers: Alters reality and matter.
- Appears In: Thor: The Dark World (as the Aether), Avengers: Infinity War (sought by Thanos)
- Key Moments: Can transform reality and create illusions or physical alterations.

POWER STONE
(Purple)
- Also known as: The Orb
- Powers: Raw destructive energy and amplification of other stones.
- Appears In: Guardians of the Galaxy (orb), Avengers: Infinity War
- Key Moments: Nearly destroyed Xandar; a major threat in the wrong hands.

TIME STONE
(Green)
- Also known as: The Eye of Agamotto
- Powers: Control over time, time loops, and temporal manipulation.
- Appears In: Doctor Strange (safeguarded by Strange), Avengers: Infinity War
- Key Moments: Used by Strange to manipulate time and to view possible futures.

SOUL STONE
(Orange)
- Also known as: Unknown history; requires sacrifice
- Powers: Unknown full capabilities; connected to soul, sacrifices.
- Appears In: Avengers: Infinity War (major reveal)
- Key Moments: Requires a grave sacrifice to obtain.

LEGEND/ICON LEGEND
- Stars/ratings and icons denote character appearances and film importance (visual icons adjacent to film entries denote main cast and key plot devices).

TIMELINE GRAFIK (selected annotations)
- Arrows and lines connect the Infinity Stones to their locations and histories through the films.
- Small circular portraits of characters are placed beside films to indicate appearances.
- Each film entry includes a small badge with a Roman numeral indicating its place in the MCU chronology.

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
The idea of a shared universe was introduced by Marvel Studios and Disney. Since 2008, the studio has released a slate of interlocking films that build toward Avengers: Infinity War. The MCU ties characters and events together across individual heroes' films, culminating in large crossover events.

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures created a cinematic universe of its own beginning with Man of Steel. The DCEU includes Superman, Batman, Wonder Woman, and more, but has taken a different approach in tone and continuity from the MCU.

20TH CENTURY FOX
Although the X-Men and Fantastic Four have long been film properties owned by 20th Century Fox, their characters have largely remained separate from the MCU due to studio rights.

SONY PICTURES
Sony retains film rights to Spider-Man and related characters. In 2015 Sony and Marvel Studios reached an agreement to allow Spider-Man to appear in the MCU while Sony continues to produce standalone Spider-Man films.

THE "FALSE" GAUNTLET
- The gauntlet used on set was a prop designed for looks and to be physically handled by actors.
- The fully functional, glowing Infinity Gauntlet seen in finished film scenes is primarily the work of visual effects artists.
- Removable stones were used for different takes; final effects added glow, power effects, and integration with Thanos' hand in post-production.

BOTTOM NOTES / SOURCES / CREDITS
- Infographic design and compilation credits (designer and/or publisher names and logos are present but small).
- Film release dates and cast lists are based on public records and film credits.
- Sources include studio publicity materials, film credits, and interviews with cast and crew.

SMALL TEXT ANNOTATIONS (examples across the timeline)
- "Starring main characters / supporting cast"
- "Abstract art indicating character relationships and film impact"
- "Key: Roman Numeral = Film number in MCU chronology"
- "Timeline shows release order with film-specific vignettes"

(Additional tiny captions and credits around margins and wheel indicate cast portraits, short blurbs about character arcs, and visual keys linking stones to films; these elaborate on the main text above.)

An educated population is necessary for a country to remain competitive in the global marketplace, but often personal and societal decisions make devoting a lot of time and money to advanced degrees difficult.

There's no doubt an education is necessary, yet when it comes to grad school, for some the cons outweigh the pros.

Who's fighting the war on grad school? // POLITICAL REFORMS

Tax Reform Act of 1986
Payments for teaching and research assistantships were made taxable income for many students

Technical and Miscellaneous Revenue Act of 1988
Restored tax-exempt treatment for employer-provided educational assistance and clarified some tuition benefits

College Cost Reduction and Access Act of 2007
Decreased interest rates on subsidized Stafford Loans for undergraduates
No change in interest rates for grad students

Budget Control Act of 2011
Graduate students lost some eligibility or saw reductions in federal funding for certain programs (cuts to higher education budgets)

Bipartisan Student Loan Certainty Act of 2013
Graduate student loan interest rates tied to market-based rates, potentially increasing borrowing costs for grad students

2018 Tax Reform
Changes to tax law affected deductions and tax treatment of some tuition benefits and related graduate student financial supports

(Each box includes short descriptions of how the law impacted graduate funding, assistantship taxation, and loan provisions)

ARE THE CRITICS RIGHT?

Reasons not to pursue a graduate education
- Wreaking havoc on relationships
  - Jeopardizing family responsibilities
  - Stressful relationship with significant other
- Opportunity cost: Graduate students must give up:
  - Travel
  - Work experience
  - Vacations
Will students regret time spent on grad school?

- Political scientist

3 Make professional connections
Access to school alumni
Develop network of peers

The private business sector, higher ed, and for-profits have weighed in and offered their two cents with tuition reimbursement and MOOCs.

Making Grad School Work

Explore financial options
Employers often offer tuition reimbursement:

- AT&T: up to $5,250 per year

- Verizon: up to $5,250 per year

Lower costs, more flexible schedules, and reduced living expenses may make online programs cheaper.

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD
MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
The films in this infographic belong to the official MCU canon and are produced by Marvel Studios. Characters and events are tied together across films leading into Avengers: Infinity War.

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures created their own shared universe including Man of Steel, Batman v Superman, Suicide Squad, Wonder Woman and Justice League.

20TH CENTURY FOX
Home of the X-Men and Fantastic Four film franchises. Although these characters are Marvel Comics creations, film rights had been licensed to Fox, meaning they existed in a separate cinematic universe to the MCU.

SONY PICTURES
Owns the film rights to Spider-Man. Sony and Marvel/Disney have a deal allowing Spider-Man to appear in the MCU films while Sony distributes standalone Spider-Man films.

The "false" gauntlet
- This gold gauntlet appeared to be Thanos' Infinity Gauntlet in promotional material, but early MCU appearances and props suggested variations. The true gauntlet and its provenance are explained in later films.
- Some replicas and props were later revealed to be decoys or hoaxes in behind-the-scenes production.

Quick facts & notes
- The Infinity Stones each have unique powers and can interact to amplify each other.
- Some Stones have been hidden, destroyed, or disguised throughout history.
- Several characters have come into contact with Stones without fully understanding them.

SOURCE / CREDITS
- Timeline, artwork and details compiled from Marvel Studios films and official materials.
- Additional research: production notes, official trailers and film credits.

column13:
Social Media Platform
Cheat Sheet

Pinterest

Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.

Best for:
Link clicks

Resource and expertise level needed:
High

Good for these business types:
Restaurants
Interior design
Wedding venues

Twitter

Key differentiating trait:
Fast-moving, real-time, news-driven platform. Strong customer service element, be prepared to engage and respond to customers here.

Best for:
Link clicks, engagement, and foot traffic

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Retail
Nonprofits
Newspapers & media outlets

LinkedIn

Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.

Best for:
Link clicks, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium

Good for these business types:
Luxury goods & services
Software providers
B2B Companies

Instagram

Key differentiating trait:
There are very few ways to link from Instagram, so this is a brand-building platform. You need to have lots of high-quality images and videos to support this platform.

Best for:
Foot traffic, word-of-mouth awareness, engagement

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium to high

Good for these business types:
Bakeries & Coffee Shops
Travel agencies
Art museums

Facebook

Key differentiating trait:
This is the platform, but it doesn't mean you need to be there. With the recent challenges the platform has faced, some users are choosing to spend their social media time elsewhere (like Instagram, which is also a Facebook platform). That being said, there's still no better place to find and engage customers, donors, or others.

Best for:
Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Local Brick & Mortar Stores
Nonprofits
B2B Companies

Constant Contact

VARIETY SHOW

Dent Corn

Flint Corn
Also called "Indian corn" due to its use and cultivation by Native Americans

Sweet Corn
A variety grown primarily for eating fresh

Popcorn
When popped, it's a popular snack at movie theaters and sporting events

Flour Corn
Composed largely of soft starch, it has easily ground, mealy kernels

Pod Corn
A variety that has several kernels enclosed in a husk

CORNUCOPIA

One of the most versatile grains, corn can be cooked, stewed, dried and ground into many different culinary creations and industrial products.

Corn Oil
Corn Syrup
Mother's OLD-FASHIONED Grits
POLENTA
ANIMAL FEED
CORN BREAD mix
Baby Corn
Corn Chowder
Hominy
Creamed Corn
Crunchy corn flakes
MILK
ETHANOL
BOURBON WHISKEY
GLUE
Pesticide?
CORN STARCH
Fresh!
POP CORN

1
Only popcorn kernels can pop. Each kernel contains a small amount of water stored in the soft starch inside the hard outer casing.

2
When heated to around 450ºF, the moisture turns to steam, creating pressure within.

3
The casing gives way and the kernel explodes, releasing steam and turning the kernel inside out. Now the popcorn is ready to eat.

A-MAIZE-ING FACTS

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in Central America, where the Olmec and Maya cultivated the tall grain in numerous varieties throughout Mesoamerica and worshiped it in the form of their Tonacatecuhtli Maize God. The Aztecs also had their own maize deities, Centeotl and Chicomecoatl.

Corn was spread through the Western Hemisphere by various Native Americans. Early European explorers were introduced to the New World grain in the 15th and 16th centuries, and later introduced it to other parts of the world because of its ability to grow in diverse climates.

The American "Corn Belt," the area of land with the highest yields and richest soils from North Dakota to Ohio. It is such a way of life in the American Midwest that even the University of Nebraska's mascot is called "The Cornhusker."

Corn's health benefits include a rich source of vitamins and nutrients, including folic acid, niacin and vitamin C. High in fiber, it is also believed to reduce the risk of colorectal cancer, hemorrhoids and heart disease.

The Corn Palace in Mitchell, South Dakota, was originally built to showcase the state's rich soil and encourage people to settle the area. It's now a popular tourist destination decorated with crop art murals and mosaics made from corn and other grains.

Sources: Betty Fussell's "The Story of Corn" / Gail Gibbons' "Corn" / Charles Micucci's "The Life and Times of Corn" / home.howstuffworks.com/corn3.htm / http://www.organicauthority.com/health-benefits/cereals/health-benefits-of-corn.html / http://mentalfloss.com/

column3: What is the third phase of Love?
column13:
M

The
SCIENCE
of
LOVE

Love at First Sight
You see someone.
Within milliseconds, your medial prefrontal cortex tells you they're attractive.

Chemical Romance
Hormones and neurochemicals from your brain flood your body.
Love comes in THREE stages, which can overlap.

1
SEXUAL DESIRE
Possible without attachment or infatuation
Can rise and fall during long-term relationships
Testosterone increases in both men and women
Estrogen increases in women

2
LIMERENCE
Infatuation
A primitive instinct
Characterized by an increase in dopamine, which motivates you to pursue a reward
Serotonin decreases, which can sometimes cause obsession
Testosterone decreases in men and increases in women
(this may help men focus sexual interest)

3
ATTACHMENT
Increases with time in healthy and sustainable relationships, while limerence dwindles
Oxytocin (the "cuddle hormone") increases over time in long-lasting relationships

Love Can Be Scary
If you see someone you find attractive, your sympathetic nervous system may have a fight-or-flight reaction, releasing adrenaline, norepinephrine and cortisol.
Adrenaline and norepinephrine make your heart race.

Broken Heart?
Losing a loved one or a love interest can also cause a stress response in the body.
For older people, disruption in routine can cause depression.

Get the Nerve Up
Your vagus nerve runs from your brain to your stomach.
Your fight-or-flight stress response stimulates this nerve, which is why your stomach does somersaults when you see a person you find attractive.

Sources:
https://www.livescience.com/24593-brain-love-dating-decisions.html
https://sitn.hms.harvard.edu/flash/2017/love-actually-science-behind-lust-attraction-companionship/
http://www.helenfisher.com/downloads/articles/10lustattraction.pdf

Northwestern Medicine

column3: Which color does 'K' represent in CMYK?
column13:
COLOR

Profiles & Printing Explained

What's the Difference?
CMYK, RGB, and Pantone are industry standards for color profiles.
How do they compare and what are their uses? Find out now!

CMYK

CMYK is the standard color profile for printing. Typically, your printer uses CYAN, MAGENTA, YELLOW, and BLACK inks to print colored images. When all colors are mixed together, a rich black is produced.

CMYK USE

CMYK is used for printed materials. CMYK colors do not appear as bright as RGB (they are missing the added bonus of a light source). Brochures, business cards, posters, etc. may be printed in CMYK.

RGB

RGB refers to the three primary colors, RED, GREEN and BLUE. RGB colors use light to make their colors bright. If you mix them all together you would get pure white.

RGB USE

RGB is the color profile used for digital screens and devices. Your screen mixes red, green, and blue light to produce the bright colors you see on your display. Websites, mobile apps, video, etc. are in RGB.

PANTONE

The Pantone Matching System (PMS) is used to correctly match materials with their corresponding RGB or CMYK colors. This insures that a color looks the same each time it is produced when matched to a Pantone swatch.

PANTONE® 247 C

PMS USE

Pantone Matching System can be used to accurately match colors for anything from fabrics and paints to t-shirts and printed materials.

What About Printing?
Knowing the differences between the color profiles can help you to understand how different printing processes work and how those processes will affect your completed project.

SPOT COLOR (OFFSET)

PANTONE® 369 C   PANTONE® 350 C

Spot color printing describes the process where specialized ink or specially blended ink is created to make a perfect match that corresponds to your desired color, such as a Pantone swatch.

If there is just one color to be printed, there will be a single plate, and a single run of the press. If there are two colors, there will be two plates and two runs, and so on. The colors are layered onto the paper one by one.

BEST FOR:
Spot color printing would be typically used for jobs which require no full color imagery, such as for business cards and other stationery, or in monotone (or duotone etc) literature such as black and white newspaper print.

* If you want something printed that has a unique color, like a metallic or fluorescent, spot color printing will be needed.

FOUR COLOR (OFFSET)

C  M  Y  K

4 Color process printing refers to the CMYK printing process. This means your artwork will be printed using only Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Black ink colors.

Just like spot color printing, each color is run through one at a time. The layering of each ink color produces a final product with a full color spectrum.

BEST FOR:
Four color process is the most common form of printing and is best for large batches, like 500 postcards. It produces clear, sharp prints.

* In some cases it may be possible to do a six-color or eight-color print. This type of process is more expensive, but will produce a wider range of colors.

DIGITAL PRINTING

Digital printing uses either toner or ink-jet technology. Digital printing might still use CMYK, but all colors are applied at once instead of with the "offset" roller method.

Although it has been considered in the past to not be as "crisp" as offset printing, technologies continue to improve and digital printing can produce some great results.

BEST FOR:
Digital printing is recommended for small batches, like 20 invitations or just one poster.

* Because digital printing does not require the creation of printing plates, turn-around time is often much faster.

The Logo Co
Taking design to new heights

Social Media & Social Change

Thanks to social media, individuals and organizations have been empowered to advance social change in education like never before. Check out how some of the pros are doing it, and brush up on a few ways you can help, too.

Stats on Supporting Social Change

73% of U.S. adults say being involved in social change is personally important to them.

Likelihood to engage in positive social change when economic conditions are bad:

U.S. adults who say they have engaged in positive social change by donating money, goods or services.

4 out of 5 adults believe "I can make the world a better place by my actions."

Making Social Change

With their ties to social media, these organizations are making it a snap for millions of people to help their causes.

KICKSTARTER

Kickstarter's all-or-nothing funding method requires projects to reach their funding goals before they receive any money. This allows people to test how much support there is for an idea. Search the site for classroom needs and projects that can be supported.
Visit:
http://www.kickstarter.com/

Twitter Followers

- 65,760 projects have been launched
- 27,240 projects have been successfully funded
- $303 million has been pledged to all projects
- $250 million has been collected, and funded projects
- 3,856 projects are currently live
- 44% success rate
- 7 projects raised more than $1 million

DonorsChoose.org

Teachers post their classroom needs, everything from pencils to microscope slides, and people can donate online to whichever need they like. Once the project reaches its funding goal, the materials are delivered to the school. Photos of the project taking place are sent to donors.

PENCILS of PROMISE

This non-profit builds schools and educates children in developing countries. Instead of spending big bucks on advertising, PoP developed one of the largest non-profit followings on social media. Folks can donate on the site or bid on artwork through their Made with Pencils campaign.

crowdrise

http://www.crowdrise.com/give

22,232

Twitter Followers
14,706

All statistics current as of August 1, 2012.

How Social Media can be used for Social Change

Don't worry, one person can make a difference in the world. Here's how:

Email
Send individual, personal emails about your cause to folks, like legislators.

Blogging
- Ask local weekly newspapers or on-topic blogs if you can write a guest post on your cause.
- Begin your own blog for your cause, and become a resource for others.
- Email a link to your post to legislators and the cause's PR people for more visibility.
- Inspire others to get fired up about your cause and possibly donate.

Facebook & Twitter
- Like the fan pages and accounts of causes you care about.
- Like and follow legislators and other people interested in these causes.
- When the causes post something you like, share it on your profile or retweet it.
- Tag legislators and others in posts about your cause so they'll stay updated too.
- Search through Facebook's causes app to connect with others interested in the same issue.

The 2012 Social Change Impact Report revealed that people do want to help out, even when times are bad

column13:
www.businessinsider.com | info@businessinsider.com | www.analyticsweek.com
Investigating
Data Scientists
Understanding their Skills, Roles and Team Makeup

A Study of 600+ Data Scientists*
Respondents provided self-assessment ratings about proficiency in 5 skill areas:
1. Business
2. Technology
3. Programming
4. Math
5. Statistics

Respondents provided information about their team makeup, job role, industry, satisfaction with work outcome, gender and more.

Most respondents were from B2B companies (80%), North America (64%). About 46% work for companies with less than 100 employees.

*Results include participants in corporate data science teams, startups and consultancies. Respondents were recruited using AnalyticsWeek newsletter, blog posts, AnalyticsWeek and social media.

Top 5 Industries Employing Data Scientists
1. Information Technology (26%)
2. Education / Science (14%)
3. Consulting (13%)
4. Financial Services (11%)
5. Healthcare & Medical (9%)

Data Scientists are Highly Educated
Most data scientists hold graduate degrees (~67%)
Nearly half of data scientists hold a Master's degree
There was no difference between males and females in their educational attainment

Data Science Skills

Top 10 Data Science Skills
1. Communication
2. Structured Data
3. Data Mining and Visualization Tools
4. Science / Scientific Method
5. Math
6. Project Management
7. Domain Expertise
8. Statistics and Statistical Modeling
9. Product Design and Development
10. Business Development

Skill Areas of Data Science
1. Subject Matter
- Business users
- Product experts
- Domain experts
- Subject-matter experts
2. Tools
- Programming (R, Python, Java)
- Data mining and visualization tools
- Databases, Hadoop, NoSQL
- Cloud platforms and big data infra
3. Statistics
- Statistics and statistical modeling
- Experimentation and A/B testing
- Machine learning and predictive models

Data Science Roles

“Researcher” is the most popular data science job role

Data Scientists are not Created Equal
- Business Managers understand business (i.e., subject matter) but not tech or stats
- Developers understand technology and programming but not business and statistics
- Creatives possess a basic knowledge in the three skill areas
- Researchers are strong in statistics and math but weak in business and programming

Four Types of Data Scientists
- Business Managers include leaders, business persons and entrepreneurs
- Developers includes developers and engineers
- Creatives includes Jack of all trades, artists and hackers
- Researchers include scientists and statisticians

Data Science is a Team Sport

Diverse Data Science Teams Perform Better
Researchers and Developers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Business experts
Business Managers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Statistics experts

Bigger Teams, Bigger Outcomes
Data scientists who work with 4 or more people on their analytics projects report better outcomes compared to their counterparts who work with fewer people.

Getting Insights from your Data

The Scientific Method
1. Formulate a question or problem statement.
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable.
3. Gather/Generate data through experiments or observation.
4. Analyze data/Draw conclusions (test hypothesis).
- e.g., test hypothesis(ies) and examine whether data supports or refutes them
5. Communicate results / Take action (e.g., change processes) based on the conclusions. Refine hypotheses for further testing.

The scientific method is a way of investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge, or correcting and integrating previous knowledge.

Three Primary Skills of Data Scientists
Data scientists bring three primary skills to bear on problems.

1. Subject Matter
Subject Matter expertise helps you know what questions you need to ask.

2. Statistics
Statistics expertise helps you apply the right analytics to answer your questions.

3. Technology
Technology expertise helps you access the data and process it quickly.

How to Apply Data Science Skills to the Scientific Method
The scientific method helps data scientists organize their collective efforts to extract insight and value from data.

Learn More about the Practice of Data Science

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?
Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
How do I reach poor households?
Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?
Why should we invest in this even riskier business?

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

1
Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2
Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3
Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

RECOMMENDATIONS

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

* All dollar amounts provided are in US dollars.
* Some figures have been rounded; please see the full publication for more details.
* Estimates reflect one time sales of improved sanitation facilities to those without access.

column3: What kind of Cholesterol is not harmful to your body?
column13:
YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

THE POWER OF BLOOD DIAGNOSTICS

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

70%

GOOD NEWS:
Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH

For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.

Approximately 41% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL

Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE HDL PRODUCTION:

Oatmeal
Fish
Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:

Cheese
Fried Foods
High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HDL/LDL LEVELS.

50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and Lp(a) gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

50%

TRIGLYCERIDES

A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:

Obesity
Cigarette Smoking
High-Carb Diet
Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION

A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS

Salmon
Blueberries
Oranges
Green Tea
Extra Virgin Olive Oil
Papayas
Sweet Potatos
Broccoli

THYROID

More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:

Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease.

Increase cold intolerance and fatigue.

Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy.

VITAMIN D

Because we are not outdoors and don't get as much sun as our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.

Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D; testing gives insight into how to achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST

OVERALL HEALTH
TIME

Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for the future.

WellnessFX™

SOURCES: THEVAPTEST.COM | NIH.GOV | RIGHTDIAGNOSIS.COM | IHEART.ORG | JOHNSHOPKINSHALERTS.COM | ADVAMED.ORG

Created by COLUMN FIVE

Social Media & Social Change

Thanks to social media, individuals and organizations have been empowered to advance social change in education like never before. Check out how some of the pros are doing it, and brush up on a few ways you can help, too.

Stats on Supporting Social Change

73% of U.S. adults say being involved in social change is personally important to them.

Making Social Change
Statistics

With their ties to social media, these organizations are making it a snap for millions of people to help their causes.

KICKSTARTER

Kickstarter's all-or-nothing funding method requires projects to reach their funding goals before they receive any money. This allows people to test how much support there is for an idea. Search the site for classroom needs and projects that can be supported.
Visit:
http://www.kickstarter.com/

Facebook Likes
318,693

Twitter Followers
552,076

- 65,760 projects have been launched
- 27,240 projects have been successfully funded
- $303 million has been pledged to all projects
- $250 million has been collected, and funded projects
- 3,856 projects are currently live
- 44% success rate
- 7 projects raised more than $1 million

DonorsChoose.org

Teachers post their classroom needs, everything from pencils to microscope slides, and people can donate online to whichever need they like. Once the project reaches its funding goal, the materials are delivered to the school. Photos of the project taking place are sent to donors.
Visit:
http://www.donorschoose.org

Facebook Likes
111,805

Twitter Followers
121,895

- $119,891,952 raised
- 6,807,024 students helped
- 1,757,293 contributions made
- 287,208 projects have been funded
- 94% of teachers said their funded projects increased their effectiveness in the classroom.
- 70% of projects get fully funded
- 65% of funded projects were for high poverty schools

PENCILS OF PROMISE

This non-profit builds schools and educates children in developing countries. Instead of spending big bucks on advertising, PoP developed one of the largest non-profit followings on social media. Folks can donate on the site or bid on artwork through their Made with Pencils campaign.
Visit:
http://www.pencilsofpromise.org/

Facebook Likes
161,821

Twitter Followers
144,718

- 4,500 children have been educated so far
- 50,000 lives have been impacted by Pencils of Promise programs
- 2,800,000 instructional hours have been completed
- 58 schools have been completed
- 12 schools are being built
- 11 schools have upcoming builds

crowdrise

Folks can start their own fundraising campaign on the site or join someone else's project team and help them. Although not dedicated to educational efforts, CrowdRise lists thousands of school related projects, charities and events.
Visit:
http://www.crowdrise.com/give

Facebook Likes
22,232

Twitter Followers
14,706

All statistics current as of August 1, 2012.

How Social Media can be used for Social Change
Don't worry, one person can make a difference in the world. Here's how:

Email
Send individual, personal emails about your cause to folks, like legislators.

Blogging
- Ask local weekly newspapers or on-topic blogs if you can write a guest post on your cause.
- Begin your own blog for your cause, and become a resource for others.
- Email a link to your post to legislators and the cause's PR people for more visibility.
- Inspire others to get fired up about your cause and possibly donate.

Facebook & Twitter
- Like the fan pages and accounts of causes you care about.
- Like and follow legislators and other people interested in these causes.
- When the causes post something you like, share it on your profile or retweet it.
- Tag legislators and others in posts about your cause so they'll stay updated too.
- Search through Facebook's causes app to connect with others interested in the same issue.

INNOVATION NEWS DAILY
www.InnovationNewsDaily.com

RISE OF THE DRONES

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are remote-controlled aircraft that can carry cameras, sensors and weapons over enemy territory. The decade since 9/11 has seen these remote aircraft increase in prominence from speculative prototypes to America's primary counterterrorism weapon.

The largest drone...

With a range of 10,000 miles (16,000 kilometers), the Northrop Grumman RQ-4 Global Hawk cruises at high altitude, loitering over an area for up to 30 hours. The drone produces high-quality surveillance images using its suite of sensors.

Allison Rolls-Royce turbofan engine has 7,050 lb (3,200 kg) thrust

Satellite communications antenna

Sensor suite includes synthetic aperture radar, electro-optical sensors and infrared sensors

Wings composed of high-strength, lightweight graphite composite material

HUMAN TO SCALE

Global Hawk cutaway drawing by Jim Picarella/FlightGlobal, used with permission

On the Battlefield: Use of Drones in Combat

1 Oct. 7, 2001: The first armed Predator drones begin flying missions over Afghanistan less than a month after 9/11. By March 2002, Drones would begin providing close air support for troops on the ground.

2 Nov. 3, 2002: A CIA-operated Predator drone launched a Hellfire missile at a car carrying five al-Qaida members. It was the first drone assassination in what would become a decadelong campaign.

3 Dec. 23, 2002: A Predator drone fires a sidewinder missile at an Iraqi fighter plane in the No Fly Zone. This is the first instance of air-to-air combat with a drone. During the surge in 2007, drone surveillance would significantly reduce the number of roadside bomb attacks.

4 May 7, 2004: The CIA begins a covert drone bombing campaign in Pakistan's tribal regions. Over the next four years, President George Bush would order 42 more drone strikes in the region. From 2008 to 2011, President Barack Obama has ordered at least 180 strikes. Some estimates put the death toll from the campaign at close to 2,000.

5 Late 2004: The U.S. Border Patrol begins testing UAVs for patrolling the U.S./Mexico border. In 2009, the U.S. begins flying drones inside of Mexican airspace in support of Mexican government antidrug cartel operations.

6 Late 2009: U.S. begins drone flights off of Somalia to monitor and deter pirates. In June 2011, the U.S. begins basing drones at the Mogadishu airport and launching drone missile strikes within Somalia against al-Shabab militants linked to al-Qaida.

7 April 21, 2011: President Obama approves the use of drones in support of the Libyan rebels. The drones destroy Qaddafi air defenses in advance of the NATO bombing campaign, and provide targeting and support for NATO allies during the war. Operations in Libya also feature the first use of drone helicopters in combat.

8 Jan. 25, 2011: A Texas Department of Public Safety SWAT team deploys a small drone during a raid on a drug stash house. This marks the first use of UAVs for domestic surveillance.

column3: what is the responsibility of mother of the groom?
column5: Plan and host the rehearsal dinner.
column13:
THE ULTIMATE
Wedding Roles
VISUAL GUIDE
WHO DOES WHAT

Choose best man and ushers
Buy the wedding ring
Splash out on the best suit you can afford
Kit out best man and ushers in matching attire
Buy a gift for your bride
Secure marriage license and pay the officiant
Get thank-you gifts for helpers, plus spares!
Prepare your speech

Select bridesmaids and maid of honour
Order flowers for hair
Shop for a wedding dress and jewellery
Buy gifts for your bridesmaids
Get present for the groom
Set aside time for a hair and make-up rehearsal
Plan a pre-wedding dinner for you and your best girls
Buy wedding ring for groom

BRIDE AND GROOM
Joint Responsibilities

Decide on budget
Choose a date and venue
Design your invite
Select flowers

Pick the band
Choose a photographer
Write thank-you letters
Write the vows

Agree on food/drink
Book your honeymoon
Hire a wedding car
Gifts for your parents

BEST MAN

Organise and host a memorable stag party
Co-ordinate suit, tie and buttonhole with the groom and ushers
Get the groom to the ceremony, and organise transport for the newly married couple
Supervise the ushers
Plan and write your speech!
Escorts maid of honour after the ceremony

MAID OF HONOUR

Plan and host the hen party
Help choose the wedding dress and bridesmaids' outfits
Organise the other bridesmaids
Help the bride get ready - and calm her nerves
Witness and sign the wedding certificate
Arrange the bride's veil and train throughout

FATHER (BRIDE/GROOM)

Pay for the rehearsal dinner (groom)
Propose the last toast at the rehearsal dinner (groom)
Co-host and help pay for the reception (bride)
Escort the bride down the aisle (bride)
Plan, write and deliver wedding speech (bride)
Pay for all drinks at the reception (groom)

MOTHER (BRIDE/GROOM)

Plan and host the rehearsal dinner (groom)
Bring the guestbook to the reception (bride)
Help the bride choose her wedding dress and jewellery (bride)
Look after the wedding gifts (bride)
Help organise ceremony & reception (bride)
Co-host the reception (bride)

USHERS

Help the best man organise the stag party
Fold and hand out order-of-service booklets at the ceremony
Distribute corsages to family members before they're seated
Direct guests to their seats
Escort the bridesmaids after the ceremony
Decorate the newlyweds' car

BRIDESMAIDS

Offer to make, decorate and distribute the wedding favours
Help the maid of honour organise hen night
Check and/or decorate the wedding venue
Run errands for the bride
Be one of the first on the dancefloor, and encourage others to join you
Walk down the aisle behind the maid of honour

Got an important wedding role to play?
Let us take some of the responsibility off your shoulders, and write your wedding speech for you
www.greatspeechwriting.co.uk

Sources:
www.hitched.co.uk
www.brideandgroom.com
www.bridalguide.com

Great Speech Writing
column15: Typically the mother of the groom helps coordinate with the mother of the bride, assists with the guest list and invitations, helps plan/host the rehearsal dinner, assists with attire and dress fittings, greets and helps seat guests on the day, may contribute financially, and often gives a toast or speech.

column13:
The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on Canadian families and children

We asked Canadians what it is like to be a parent during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Here are some of the participants’ responses:

Screen time, reading and physical activity were the most popular daily activities

Daily or almost every day

Screen time
89%

Reading books or stories
62%

Physical activities
61%

Games
23%

Music, drama or visual arts
21%

Developing other skills
18%

Percentage of participants who said that their children engaged in selected activities at home

Parents' top concerns were children's opportunities to socialize with friends and balancing child care, schooling and work

Percentage of participants who were very or extremely concerned about their children's...

71% Opportunities to socialize with friends
64% Amount of screen time
54% Loneliness or isolation
46% General mental health
40% School year and academic success

Percentage of participants who were very or extremely concerned for their families in terms of...

Balancing child care, schooling and work 74%
Managing their child's or children's behaviours, stress levels, anxiety and emotions 61%
Having less patience, raising their voice, or scolding or yelling at their children 46%
Staying connected with family or friends 43%
Getting along and supporting each other 37%
Feeling lonely in their own home 30%

Parents of young school-aged children were the most likely to be very or extremely concerned about balancing child care, schooling and work

Preschool-aged children only 70%
School-aged children aged 4 to 11 80%
School-aged children aged 12 to 14 55%
Children of various ages 76%

For more information, please check out the Daily article.

Timeline
Spices were among the most valuable items of trade in ancient and medieval times,
they were rare and precious products, used for medicine, perfume, incense, and flavoring and played a big role throughout human history.

3500 BC

were using various spices for
flavouring food, in cosmetics,
and for embalming their dead.

THE LIFESPAN OF STORAGE MEDIA

FOR AS LONG WE HAVE BEEN INVENTING WAYS TO CAPTURE AND PRESENT DATA,

we have been developing media on which to store it. With each new innovation, we've seen greater convenience, increased storage capacity, and longer lifespans. But at some point, even with the utmost care, media fails... leaving us with a useless piece of plastic, tape, or metal.

The Cloud, with its extensive network of server farms, leverages redundant storage media, ensuring that in the event of hardware failure, the data lives on, stored safely in another part of the cloud. column13:
WHY FREEZE-DRIED FOOD?

Originally produced for the U.S. Military and NASA, freeze-dried food has several advantages over other dehydrated foods.

LONG SHELF-LIFE

Uses two different types of packaging for freeze-dried food

The food can be stored at room temperature without refrigeration

EASY PREPARATION

Freeze-dried products can be rehydrated (reconstituted) very quickly and easily

All that is required is water. Although hot water is recommended for the fastest reconstitution time (8-10 minutes) and the best taste

CONVENIENT STORAGE

98% of the water is removed from our foods, making them incredibly light, portable, and compact

3-month supply of food for a family of 5 can easily be stored under a twin-sized bed.

freeze drying process

GOURMET QUALITY

it's food that not only you but your kids will want to eat as well

The food tastes like actual homemade meals, not something that came out of a bunker

NUTRITIOUS

The freeze dried process does not cause the same damage or toughening to the food as other processes do

HOT    25 Years    COOL

Can retain the same nutritional value, taste, texture and color for over 25 years when stored in a cool dry place

FRESH AND DELICIOUS

Australian Government

COVIDSAFE

3 STEP FRAMEWORK FOR A COVIDSAFE AUSTRALIA

MAINTAIN 1.5M DISTANCING AND GOOD HYGIENE • STAY HOME IF UNWELL • FREQUENTLY CLEAN AND DISINFECT COMMUNAL AREAS • COVIDSAFE PLAN FOR WORKPLACES AND PREMISES

ALL STEPS ARE SUBJECT TO EXPERT HEALTH ADVICE • States and territories can implement changes based on their COVID-19 conditions

GATHERINGS & WORK
EDUCATION & CHILDCARE
RETAIL & SALES
CAFES & RESTAURANTS
ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES
SPORT & RECREATION
ACCOMMODATION
WEDDINGS, FUNERALS & RELIGIOUS SERVICES
HAIR & BEAUTY SERVICES
DOMESTIC TRAVEL

STEP 1
STEP 1: The important first small steps - connect with friends and family - allowing groups of people to be together in homes and in the community. Businesses reopen, and more people return to work

GATHERINGS & WORK (Step 1)
Non-work gatherings of up to 10
Up to 5 visitors or more in addition to the normal residents
Work from home if it works for you and your employer
Workplaces develop a COVIDSafe plan
Avoid public transport in peak hour

EDUCATION & CHILDCARE (Step 1)
Child care centres, primary and secondary schools open as per state and territory plans
Universities/technical colleges to increase face-to-face where possible and prioritise hands-on, skills based learning

RETAIL & SALES (Step 1)
Retail stores open
Retail stores and shopping centre managers must develop COVIDSafe plans
Auctions/open homes can have gatherings of up to 10, recording contact details

CAFES & RESTAURANTS (Step 1)
May open and seat up to 10 patrons at one time
Need to maintain an average density of 4m² per person
Food courts are to remain closed for seated patrons

ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES (Step 1)
To remain closed: indoor cinemas, concert venues, stadia, galleries, museums, zoos, pubs, registered and licensed clubs, nightclubs, gambling venues, strip clubs and brothels

SPORT & RECREATION (Step 1)
No indoor physical activity including gyms
Community centres, outdoor gyms, playgrounds and skate parks allowed up to 10 people
Outdoor sport (up to 10 people) consistent with the AIS Framework for Rebooting Sport
Pools open with restrictions

ACCOMMODATION (Step 1)
Continue current arrangements for caravan parks and camping grounds (closed to tourists in some states and territories)
Hostels and hotels are open for accommodation

WEDDINGS, FUNERALS & RELIGIOUS SERVICES (Step 1)
Weddings may have up to 10 guests in addition to the couple and the celebrant
Funerals may have up to 20 mourners outdoors and 10 indoors
Religious gatherings may have up to 10 attendees
Every gathering must record contact details

HAIR & BEAUTY SERVICES (Step 1)
Hairdressers and barber shops open and record contact details
Beauty therapy and massage therapy services, saunas and tattoo parlours remain closed

DOMESTIC TRAVEL (Step 1)
Allow local and regional travel for recreation
Refer to state and territory governments for border restrictions and biosecurity conditions

STEP 2
STEP 2: Building on slightly larger gatherings and more businesses reopening. Higher risk activities may have tighter restrictions

GATHERINGS & WORK (Step 2)
Non-work gatherings of up to 20
States and territories may allow larger numbers in some circumstances
Work from home if it works for you and your employer
Workplaces develop a COVIDSafe plan
Avoid public transport in peak hour

EDUCATION & CHILDCARE (Step 2)
Child care centres, primary and secondary schools open as per state and territory plans
Universities/technical colleges to increase face-to-face where possible and prioritise hands-on, skills based learning

RETAIL & SALES (Step 2)
Retail stores open
Retail stores and shopping centre managers must develop COVIDSafe plans
Auctions/open homes can have gatherings of up to 20, recording contact details

CAFES & RESTAURANTS (Step 2)
Cafes and restaurants can seat up to 20 patrons at one time
Need to maintain an average density of 4m² per person
Food courts are to remain closed for seated patrons

ENTERTAINMENT & AMUSEMENT VENUES (Step 2)
Indoor movie theatres, concert venues, museums, galleries, stadia may open with restrictions

TOP 5 REASONS WHY EXECUTIVES VISIT ONLINE NETWORKS:
- Great way to keep track of peers and colleagues
- Easy access to thought leadership & information that can't be found elsewhere
- Easy way to showcase themselves or their companies
- Easy access to learning and professional development
- They want to find out what others think of products, vendors or approaches

EXECUTIVES MAY BE HESITANT TOWARDS SOCIAL MEDIA BECAUSE:

1. THEY WANT CONTROL.
The beauty of social media lies in the fact that it's an inherently open and transparent medium of communication. You never know who will be reached and how people will respond.

2. LACK OF UNDERSTANDING.
Technology and digital innovation is rapidly changing and CEOs feel vulnerable when they don't know exactly how something works - so many of them they stay away altogether.

3. THEY FEAR IT WILL BE JUST A FAD.
Without a clear vision of the future direction of social media and how it can be strategically and sustainably utilized to benefit the company, CEOs opt not to put energy into such uncharted waters.

USE CMYK COLOR MODE

CMYK (Cyan-Magenta-Yellow-Black)
- Subtractive color model
- Refers to the 4 colors used in the printing process
- Smaller range of colors available than in RGB Mode
- Files must be saved in CMYK mode in order to be printable

RGB (Colors for Web)

RGB (Red-Green-Blue)
- Additive color model
- Red, Green and Blue colors are added together by light to create any color
- Any RGB file will automatically be converted to CMYK format when the files are prepared for printing
- During RGB to CMYK file conversion there is often a loss of color vibrance
- For consistency create design work in CMYK mode from start to finish

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS
... The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.
... Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.
... The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness."
... In the 4th century C.E., Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. column13:
Essay

War Literature

The Art of War

Author
Sun Tzu

Years Written
c. 475–221 BCE

Original Language
Chinese

CONTEXT

A Battle Won Before It Is Fought

Likely written during the violent and chaotic Warring States period of Chinese history (475–221 BCE), The Art of War offers a series of military strategies to wage war, involving such tactics as careful planning before battle. The strategies and tactics are analyzed and elaborated on by 13 commentators.

The Tao
Sun Tzu was influenced by Taoist philosophy, which emphasizes balance, virtuous choices, and harmony with nature.

Warring States Period
Sun Tzu lived during a turbulent period of military conflict among China's provinces.

Chinese War Literature
The Art of War was the first of the seven military classics in Chinese literature.

Key Figures

Li Ch'uan
T'ang dynasty writer of military strategy; prominent commentator

Ts'o Ts'o
Ruthless and effective King of Wei; senior commentator

Tu Mu
High-ranking government administrator; important commentator

Mei Yao-ch'en
Song dynasty poet and writer; major commentator and historian

The Art of War by the Numbers

3:1
Desired ratio of troops to commanding officers according to Sun Tzu

254
Years during the Chinese Warring States period

1772
Year The Art of War became available to the West when it was translated and published by a French Jesuit priest

24
Members of a U.S. spy plane crew freed from detention in China in 2001 after officials used The Art of War to come up with a diplomatic strategy

Main Ideas

Predecided Outcomes
Good resources, training, and strategy are the keys to victory.

Changing Tactics
War is unpredictable, a fact that good leaders use to their advantage.

Strengths & Weaknesses
Wise commanders know theirs and their enemies' strengths and weaknesses.

Deceit as Strategy
Fooling the enemy into making miscalculations wins battles.

A victorious army wins its victories before seeking battle.
Sun Tzu, Chapter 4

Author

SUN TZU
C. 400–301 BCE

An experienced professional tactician and strategist, Sun Tzu fought for the state of Wu during a time when China's states fought one another for primacy. He formulated his 13 topics of military strategy as an organized method of conducting war with the best possible outcome.

Sources: Deciphering Sun Tzu: How to Read The Art of War by Derek M. C. Yuen, Encyclopaedia Britannica, History.com

column3: Which social media site was invented in 2002?
column13:
THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platform's , every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING
Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?".

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT
ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect Universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately the letters "LO" weren't the complete 'LOGIN' they had planned to send over.

1969

START OF SOCIAL SITES
From 1997 to 2001, Six Degrees had a peak of about 1 million members who were able to create profiles and 'friend' each other.

1997
sixdegrees™

CREATIVITY AND STATUS BLOGS
LiveJournal was a platform where users could blog as a form of keeping friends updated with their lives, a format later used by Facebook for their status'.

1999

A CHALLENGER EMERGES
Friendster, one of the first social media sites, is created and launched by Peter Chin, Jonathan Abrams, and David Lee. Within the next year, they raised $3M million dollars from Google, widely considered one of the biggest blunders of Silicon Valley.

2002
friendster®

LINK UP WITH BUSINESSES
LinkedIn gave users a place to post their resume and private message people with a business-orientated mind. It's now a very popular site for businesses to recruit or for users to search for new employees. By 2004 the number of users on the site would reach 1 million, 3 years later they would break 500 million users.

2003

GMAIL RECEIVED
Google creates its own email service, Gmail, in January. In the UK Gmail was branded as Google Mail, due to a trademark dispute, until 2009 when they changed the branding back after the dispute was over.

2004

FACEBOOK BEGINS
In February 2004, Facebook was launched by Mark Zuckerberg, along with his Harvard roommates. The site was initially limited to Harvard students but in the following months and years, this membership would be worldwide rocketing the number of users to around 2.2 billion in 14 years.

2004

BIRTH OF YOUTUBE
YouTube, a platform on which users can upload and share videos, was made public in February 2005. The first video to hit one million views was made public within a year, thanks to a Nike advert featuring Ronaldo. Facebook is now made available in the UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia and New Zealand on October 1st.

2005

MYSPACE IS BOOMING
Myspace became the most popular social network in the US during 2006! That's just 3 years after it was founded.

Twitter is launched publically on July 15th

2006

750 TWEETS PER SECOND!
65 million tweets a day are being sent out on Twitter, that's around 750 tweets every second.

2010

LAUNCH OF SNAPCHAT
Snapchat, an image messaging app, is launched in September after being tested as an iOS only app known as Picaboo in July. One of the founding members is said to have pushed out before Snapchat is released.

2011

SHORT VIDEOS, MEGA VIEWS
Vine, a video hosting service specialising in short videos, goes public in June. Twitter acquires Vine for $30 million in the October of that year.

2013
vine

UK'S INSTA ADS
Instagram brings sponsored Ads to target the UK in September following their inclusion on the app in America.

2014

LIFE OF A STARMAN
NASA's #WeAreInSpace started, giving Twitter followers updates on Scott Kelly an astronaut who lived in space for a year!

2015

VR AND FAKE NEWS
Virtual Reality, live videos, and 360-degree videos reignited all video content on social media sites like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram.
There was a crackdown on fake news throughout social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter.

2018

After all of this, we now have a beautiful array of social media channels to pick and choose from; Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Snapchat and more. Who knows what advancement social media creators will think up. What do you think will happen in the future world of social media?

www.redpill.co

column13:
A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we’re on a path to a 4°C warmer world by the end of this century. The social, economic, and environmental consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart solutions, warming can still be held below 2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age  →  Pre-industrial times (around 1750)

Since the last Ice Age to pre-industrial times (thousands of years ago):

The global mean temperature rose by 4–5°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre-industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)  →  Today

Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.

The global mean temperature is 0.8°C higher.

Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Antarctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.

Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man-made climate change.

Today  →  Future

If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.

Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 m

By 2100:
4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

There would be larger impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.

Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.

Flooding and rising sea-level hotspots

Heatwaves

Sea level rise is likely to be 15–20% higher in the tropics than the global mean

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
2°C warmer — Growth slows down
2.4°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forest fires could double
3–4°C warmer — Large sections transform to savanna

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual runoff decreases by 20–40% in the Danube, Mississippi, Amazon, and Murray-Darling Basins
All above changes likely to double

What can we do?

The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
- Make economic growth green and inclusive
- Scale up affordable technology solutions
- Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

Increasing finances to fight climate change

World Bank lending in 2012:
$7.1B
Mitigation

$4.6B
Adaptation

Making development climate–smart

Higher yields, greater resilience, and lower emissions on ~450,000 hectares

Transport
Nigeria

Energy
Bangladesh
Solar panels for off-grid homes
1.4 million low-income rural homes have electricity

Water

Integrating water resource management with pollution control
20 million+ people benefit

Policy

Development Policy Loan
Launching transformative actions in energy, water, industry, and tourism in Himachal Pradesh

Risk Analysis

Decision-making under uncertainty
Helping policy-makers make the right decisions on infrastructure in Ho Chi Minh

To learn more, go to climatechange.worldbank.org
Visit the World Bank on Facebook, and follow us on twitter @wbclimatechange

TAKEAWAYS

Keep it Simple: Positive Stories from Personal Friends Spark Purchase.
It’s clear that friends and family members prove to be the most influential drivers of purchase. While celebrities and professional bloggers may carry a huge network reach that is attractive to many brands, it is often the personal story from a trusted friend that influences action.

Facebook Friends Remain Critical.
While more networks like Pinterest and YouTube are increasing their reputation as trusted and reliable sources of information about products and services, Facebook still holds strong as your best bet for social reach and a reliable source of information about products and services.

Develop Advocate Strategies Around Each Purchase Stage.
Product recommendations and reviews are leveraged at every stage of a consumer’s purchase cycle. Consumer use different online platforms to satisfy their needs at each step — be it discovery, research or advocacy. It’s important to seed conversation that’s relevant to the consumer.

column3: How many food items listed promotes LDL?
column13:
YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

THE POWER OF BLOOD DIAGNOSTICS

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

70%

GOOD NEWS:

Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH

For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.

Approximately 41% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL

Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE HDL PRODUCTION:

Oatmeal
Fish
Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:

Cheese
Fried Foods
High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HDL/LDL LEVELS.

50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and Lp(a) gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

50%

TRIGLYCERIDES

A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:

Obesity
Cigarette Smoking
High-Carb Diet
Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION

A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS

Salmon
Blueberries
Oranges
Green Tea
Extra Virgin Olive Oil
Papayas
Sweet Potatos
Broccoli

THYROID

More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:

Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease.

Increase cold intolerance and fatigue.

Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy.

VITAMIN D

Because we are not outdoors and don't get as much sun as our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

75%

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.

Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D; testing gives insight into how to achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST

OVERALL HEALTH
TIME

Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for the future.

WellnessFX™

SOURCES: THEVAPTEST.COM | NIH.GOV | RIGHTDIAGNOSIS.COM | IHEART.ORG | JOHNSHOPKINSHEALTHALERTS.COM | ADVAMED.ORG

Created by COLUMN FIVE

column5: Tape your paper to the desk.
column13:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work

don't keep your ink right next to your paper - if you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks. try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

use your brush to pull a horizontal stripe of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

adgh

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line near the bottom of the ruled area. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to work out the size and spacing of your letters. Hold your pen at the angle you'll use for writing and draw a series of horizontal nib-width strokes down the left hand side of your page to form a ladder of nib-heights.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height is about 4-5 nib heights. Using your ruler and the nib ladder, measure up from the baseline and draw a horizontal line to mark the x-height.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw the ascender and descender lines. These mark the top of ascenders and the bottom of descenders.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It sits above the x-height and below the ascender line. Use this to keep your uppercase letters consistent.

WARNING
If you're drawing your nib ladder with your paper flat on a table your ruler may slip. Use sticky tape to hold the ruler in place, or tape the paper to the desk for extra stability.
We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker for practising before using your dip nib or brush pen - the marker will give you great control.

SOME LETTERS TO GET YOU STARTED

Hold your pen at a 30° angle for this alphabet

column13:
BUSINESS ANALYTICS vs DATA SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.
To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

BUSINESS ANALYSTS                DATA SCIENTISTS
30% Bachelor's                   28%
56% Master's                     48%
11% Doctoral                     20%

Sample Undergraduate Majors
- Business Administration            - Mathematics
- Information Technology             - Computer Science
- Finance                            - Information Science
- Political Science                  - Computer Software Engineering
- Anthropology                       - Technical Communications
- Economics                          - Computer Information Systems
- History                            - Statistics
- Psychology

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master’s degree in the field.

Decision-Making
Business Analysts:
Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.

Data Scientists:
Discover the opportunity in the datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making processes.

Applied Problem Solving
Business Analysts:
Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.

Data Scientists:
Create the foundation of analysis by which an applied business problem can be solved.

Data Analysis
Business Analysts:
Research, interpret, and visualize raw data using predictive, prescriptive and descriptive analytics to make it useful and accessible for business users.

Data Scientists:
Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multilinear regression selection.

Analytical Models
Business Analysts:
Understand, integrate, and prescribe solutions that use data modeling.

Data Scientists:
Experienced statistical programmers using languages and tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and Knime.

Database Management
Business Analysts:
Define and align database requirements for various forms of data, coded and not coded, using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Data Scientists:
Design and structure databases using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Where Do They Work?

8% State and local government, excluding education and hospitals
8% Management of companies and enterprises

column13:
HOW SOCIAL MEDIA PHOTOS AFFECT YOUR JOB SEARCH

4 REASONS YOUR PROFILE PHOTO MATTERS

90% More than 90% of employers are recruiting through social media, including LinkedIn.

94%
65%
55%
18%

Recruiters spend an average of 6 seconds per LinkedIn profile-- and 19% of that time looking at the profile picture.

Princeton researchers have found that people form an impression of you from your photo in 1/10 of a second.

IF YOU WANT THE JOB, YOU HAVE TO LOOK THE PART. RECRUITERS CONNECT WITH THIS INSTANTLY, AND ONLY THEN WILL THEY GIVE YOUR SKILLS AND CREDENTIALS THE FULL WEIGHT THEY DESERVE.

FACTORS THAT CREATE YOUR WINNING PROFILE PHOTO

RESEARCH SAYS THAT DIFFERENT PHOTOS OF THE SAME PERSON LEAVE IMPRESSIONS AS VARIED AS THOSE OF ENTIRELY DIFFERENT PEOPLE.

43
Every person has 43 distinct facial muscles.

7
There are 7 universal facial expressions: happiness, sadness, surprise, fear, anger, contempt and disgust.

10,000
When you begin to mix these facial expressions together, the human face can make more than 10,000 slightly different expressions.

NOW ADD MATTERS OF DRESS, GROOMING, LIGHT, CAMERA ANGLES, AND CAMERA DISTORTION/EDITING TO THIS COMPLICATION OF MICROEXPRESSIONS, AND YOU SEE HOW EVERY PHOTO TELLS A UNIQUE STORY.

HOW DO CORONAVIRUS DIAGNOSTIC TESTS WORK?
(aka "Do I have / Did I have COVID-19?")

A Molecular Testing
VIRUS-DERIVED

Nasal swab    SARS-CoV-2    RNA    DNA

• DNA derived from patient samples is repeatedly copied and amplified

Light
[graph]
DNA Copies → 
gene 1
gene 2
control

• Different colored dyes bind to genes for pieces of the virus.
• Another dye detects genes from related virus families.

Pros
• Early detection
• Quick processing (hours)
• Highly selective and specific

Cons
• Skilled personnel and equipment necessary
• Does not tell you if virus is still active
• Cannot detect those who've been infected and later recovered

B Serological Testing
BLOOD-DERIVED

SARS-CoV-2 infects a patient

After 2 days:
Your spleen produces IgM, one of the first antibodies to attack coronavirus

9-11 days:
More tailored antibodies called IgG are produced

As the patient's immune system kicks in, the antibodies are measured using a technique called ELISA:

Antibody
Concentration

[graph]
Light

Pros
• Rapid test available at point-of-care
• Low cost
• Can be used to test large populations
• Reliably detects exposure

Cons
• Cannot detect if patient is contagious or infection is still present
• Prone to false negatives if patient has deferred immune response
